refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. Splitting these into atomic FRs allows independent verification and clearer traceability to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that are traceable to input parameters and stakeholder requirements.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from worksite mission inputs.
FR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance targets in a retrievable format.
FR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance target baselines and calibration updates.
FR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall establish traceability between worksite inputs and derived performance targets.

candidate_dps: DP1: Capture worksite mission context (operational objectives, environmental constraints, safety policies) via structured intake process; parse into a normalized context model; apply context-to-performance mapping rules to derive candidate performance targets; validate targets against safety/policy constraints; document traceability chain from context inputs to each target.

DP2: Maintain a parameterized performance baseline repository indexed by worksite context signature; upon new mission input, query repository for similar historical baselines; apply delta-adjustment heuristics to derive candidate targets; cross-check against current safety policies; log all adjustments with rationale and timestamp.

DP3: Decompose worksite mission into functional sub-goals (e.g., throughput, safety margin, energy efficiency); assign performance metrics to each sub-goal via a decision matrix weighted by operational priority; aggregate sub-goal metrics into system-level targets; maintain audit trail of weighting decisions and policy constraints applied.

DP4: Implement a two-stage derivation: (Stage 1) Extract mission context and safety constraints as input vectors; (Stage 2) Apply a deterministic rule engine that maps context vectors to performance target ranges; store rule provenance and constraint sources; version all rules and maintain rollback capability for baseline updates.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Implement a two-stage derivation: (Stage 1) Extract mission context and safety constraints as input vectors; (Stage 2) Apply a deterministic rule engine that maps context vectors to performance target ranges; store rule provenance and constraint sources; version all rules and maintain rollback capability for baseline updates.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between rule engine and context model schema—mitigated by defining context as a stable, versioned interface contract.
Dependency on safety policy data freshness—mitigated by explicit constraint versioning and audit trail.
Rule maintenance burden if context dimensionality grows—mitigated by modular rule organization (one rule set per context dimension or mission class).
coupling_check.notes: DP4 is selected because it explicitly addresses the acceptance criteria (documented traceability, history of baselines/calibration updates) through rule versioning and provenance tracking. The two-stage separation (context extraction vs. rule application) reduces coupling compared to DP1 (which conflates parsing and mapping) and DP2 (which relies on heuristic similarity, reducing determinism). DP3 is valid but less explicit about traceability and baseline history. DP4 supports independent evolution of context schema and rule logic.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context can be reliably captured and normalized into a stable input vector schema. Risk: If context is highly unstructured or domain-specific, normalization may lose critical nuance. Mitigation: Define context schema iteratively with domain experts; include free-text fields for context not yet formalized.

Assumption: A deterministic rule engine can adequately map context to performance targets without requiring real-time optimization or learning. Risk: If worksite conditions are highly dynamic or novel, static rules may become stale. Mitigation: Implement rule versioning and periodic review cycles; log rule mismatches for continuous improvement.

Assumption: Safety policies and operational constraints are available in machine-readable form or can be reliably extracted. Risk: Policies may be implicit, evolving, or embedded in organizational culture. Mitigation: Establish a policy registry and governance process; require explicit sign-off on policy inputs to the rule engine.

Assumption: Baseline history and calibration updates can be stored and retrieved efficiently. Risk: Data volume or schema evolution may complicate historical queries. Mitigation: Use immutable audit logs with versioned schemas; implement time-series indexing for baseline queries.

Assumption: Traceability from context input to performance target is a primary quality attribute. Risk: Over-documentation may introduce overhead or brittleness. Mitigation: Automate traceability capture at rule execution time; use structured logging rather than manual documentation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP4 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Context Intake & Normalization: Define a stable, versioned schema for worksite mission context (objectives, constraints, safety policies). (2) Rule Engine: Implement a modular, versioned rule set that maps context vectors to performance target ranges; each rule must cite its source (policy, historical baseline, engineering standard). (3) Traceability & History: Maintain an immutable audit log linking each derived target to its input context, applicable rules, and policy constraints; support rollback to prior baselines. (4) Validation Gate: Before finalizing targets, cross-check against current safety policies and operational constraints; flag conflicts for manual review. This revision clarifies the IM boundaries (context intake module, rule engine module, audit/history module, validation module) and ensures that each module can be independently tested and evolved.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for deriving, managing, and enforcing safety-critical performance targets through deterministic rule engines, constraint mapping, and versioned rule provenance tracking.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission context, vehicle state, and environmental data as input vectors for Stage 1 extraction and rule engine evaluation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives task directives and mission parameters that inform safety constraint extraction and context vector formation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived performance target ranges, constraint violations, and rule provenance metadata to downstream safety enforcement and teleoperation systems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports distributed rule versioning, rollback coordination, and baseline update synchronization across system instances if multi-node deployment is required.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: rule engine, constraint mapping, versioning, and rollback are core safety service responsibilities. Stage 1 context extraction and Stage 2 deterministic mapping are naturally co-located. Minor diffusion: telemetry ingestion and alert emission are external dependencies, but these are well-defined interface contracts.

Moderate fit if NGHE is a top-level orchestration layer. Rule engine logic would be contained, but mission context extraction and safety constraint sourcing would diffuse into Vehicle and Teleoperation modules. Versioning and rollback would require cross-module coordination, increasing coupling.

Poor fit: Vehicle is typically a physical/hardware abstraction layer. Placing a deterministic rule engine and constraint derivation logic here violates separation of concerns. Rule provenance, versioning, and rollback are safety governance concerns, not vehicle control concerns. High diffusion: mission context and safety constraints would need to flow in from external sources.

Weak fit: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command execution layer. While it consumes performance targets, it should not own the rule engine or constraint derivation logic. Placing versioning and rollback here couples safety governance to operator interaction, creating unnecessary complexity and diffusion of safety logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of DP4 because: (1) deterministic rule engines for constraint mapping are inherently safety-critical and belong in a dedicated safety module; (2) rule provenance, versioning, and rollback are governance and audit concerns central to safety assurance; (3) Stage 1 context extraction and Stage 2 rule application form a cohesive safety derivation pipeline; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide clean boundaries for input/output and coordination without requiring Safety Services to own upstream or downstream logic.
containment_risks: Risk: If mission context extraction (Stage 1) requires deep domain knowledge from Vehicle or Teleoperation modules, Safety Services may need to import complex context-building logic, increasing coupling. Mitigation: Define a standardized context vector schema at the Telemetry interface to decouple context formation from rule application.

Risk: Rollback and versioning coordination across distributed nodes (if applicable) may require tight synchronization via Network Link, introducing latency or consistency challenges. Mitigation: Implement eventual consistency for rule updates with explicit versioning epochs and fallback to last-known-good baseline.

Risk: If SafetyAlert interface is consumed by multiple downstream modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE), Safety Services becomes a critical hub. Changes to alert format or semantics could cascade. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert as a versioned contract with deprecation policies.

Risk: Stage 1 context extraction may require real-time filtering or preprocessing of Telemetry data. If this preprocessing is complex, it may be better owned by a dedicated data pipeline module rather than Safety Services. Mitigation: If preprocessing is non-trivial, propose a separate 'Context Extraction' module upstream of Safety Services, with Safety Services consuming pre-formed context vectors.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP4 is well-aligned with Safety Services as proposed. However, to further improve containment and reduce diffusion, consider refining DP4 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) DP4a: 'Extract and normalize mission context and safety constraints into standardized input vectors via Telemetry and Tasking interfaces'; (2) DP4b: 'Apply deterministic rule engine to map normalized context vectors to performance target ranges, with rule provenance, versioning, and rollback capability.' This decomposition allows DP4a to be owned by a lightweight 'Context Normalization' module (or embedded in Telemetry ingestion) and DP4b to remain in Safety Services, reducing the cognitive load on Safety Services and improving testability. If context extraction is simple (direct pass-through), keep DP4 unified in Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly addresses the FR requirement to derive performance requirements from worksite mission context. The two-stage approach (Stage 1: extract mission context and safety constraints; Stage 2: apply deterministic rule engine) is logically sound and traceable. Rule provenance, versioning, and rollback capability satisfy the acceptance criterion for documented, traceable performance targets with baseline history. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation without over-specifying.

diffusion_risks: Stage 1 context extraction may diffuse if mission context formation requires deep coupling to Vehicle or Teleoperation domain knowledge. Mitigation: Enforce a standardized context vector schema at the Telemetry interface to decouple context formation from rule application logic.

Rollback and versioning coordination across distributed nodes introduces potential latency and consistency challenges via Network Link. Mitigation: Implement eventual consistency with explicit versioning epochs and fallback to last-known-good baseline rather than strict synchronous coordination.

SafetyAlert interface acts as a critical hub consumed by multiple downstream modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE). Changes to alert format or semantics risk cascading impacts. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert as a versioned contract with explicit deprecation policies and backward compatibility guarantees.

Stage 1 context preprocessing (filtering, normalization) may be non-trivial and could be better owned by a dedicated upstream module rather than Safety Services. If preprocessing is complex, propose a separate 'Context Normalization' module to reduce Safety Services cognitive load.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The module is the natural owner of DP4 because: (1) deterministic rule engines and constraint mapping are inherently safety-critical and belong in a dedicated safety module; (2) rule provenance, versioning, and rollback are governance and audit concerns central to safety assurance; (3) Stage 1 and Stage 2 form a cohesive safety derivation pipeline; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide clean boundaries without requiring Safety Services to own upstream or downstream logic. Minor diffusion arises from telemetry ingestion and alert emission as external dependencies, but these are well-defined interface contracts. Avoid placing DP4 in Vehicle (0.4 containment, 0.6 diffusion—violates separation of concerns), Teleoperation (0.45 containment, 0.55 diffusion—couples safety governance to operator interaction), or NGHE orchestration layer (0.65 containment, 0.35 diffusion—requires cross-module coordination for versioning and rollback).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Safety Services module boundary is well-defined with clear input/output interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Acceptance criteria are directly verifiable: (1) documented set of performance targets—rule engine outputs with provenance metadata; (2) traceability to worksite inputs—context vectors sourced from Telemetry and Tasking; (3) baseline history—versioning and rollback capability. Test harness can inject mission context vectors, verify rule application determinism, validate provenance records, and confirm rollback restores prior baselines. Traceability from worksite safety plan and operational policies to rule definitions can be audited via rule provenance metadata.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to Safety Services with explicit responsibility for deriving performance requirements from mission context. The allocation is unambiguous: Safety Services owns the derivation logic (DP4), while Telemetry and Tasking provide input vectors and Vehicle/Teleoperation consume outputs. No competing allocations or ownership ambiguity. However, clarity could be further improved by explicitly documenting whether Stage 1 context extraction is owned by Safety Services or delegated to an upstream Context Normalization module, depending on complexity of mission context formation.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP4 into two sub-DPs to improve separation of concerns: (1) DP4a: 'Extract and normalize mission context and safety constraints into standardized input vectors via Telemetry and Tasking interfaces'; (2) DP4b: 'Apply deterministic rule engine to map normalized context vectors to performance target ranges, with rule provenance, versioning, and rollback capability.' If context extraction is simple (direct pass-through), keep DP4 unified in Safety Services. If complex, propose a lightweight 'Context Normalization' module upstream of Safety Services.

Define a formal context vector schema at the Telemetry interface to decouple context formation from rule application. Document required fields, data types, and validation rules to enable independent testing of Stage 1 and Stage 2.

Establish SafetyAlert as a versioned contract with explicit deprecation policies and backward compatibility guarantees. Document the alert schema, versioning strategy, and migration path for downstream consumers.

Specify rollback and versioning strategy for distributed deployments: clarify whether rollback is synchronous (all nodes must revert simultaneously) or eventual (nodes converge to last-known-good baseline). Document consistency guarantees and failure modes.

Add acceptance criterion: 'Rule provenance metadata includes source constraints (worksite safety plan, operational policies), rule version, timestamp, and audit trail of baseline updates.' This strengthens traceability to worksite inputs.

Document the interface contract for Tasking input: specify which task directives and mission parameters inform safety constraint extraction, and define the schema for constraint vectors derived from Tasking.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the gate results expose two critical decomposition opportunities that warrant next-level sub-FRs: (1) Stage 1 context extraction and normalization is flagged as a potential diffusion risk and candidate for upstream ownership or explicit sub-module design; (2) versioning and rollback coordination for distributed deployments requires detailed specification of consistency semantics and failure modes. The recommended revisions suggest decomposing DP4 into DP4a (context extraction) and DP4b (rule application), which implies corresponding sub-FRs. Given high safety criticality, explicit decomposition of these concerns into verifiable sub-requirements is justified to reduce implementation ambiguity and ensure traceability. Stopping here would leave Stage 1 context extraction under-specified and create risk of diffusion during implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Extract and normalize mission context and safety constraints from worksite telemetry and tasking inputs into standardized context vectors
FR4.2: Apply deterministic rule engine to map normalized context vectors to performance target ranges with rule provenance, versioning, and rollback capability
FR4.3: Coordinate versioning and rollback of performance targets across distributed Safety Services nodes with eventual consistency guarantees
FR4.4: Establish SafetyAlert output contract with versioning, deprecation policies, and backward compatibility guarantees for downstream consumers

revise.why: Refine DP4 into two explicit sub-DPs to improve separation of concerns and reduce implementation ambiguity: (1) DP4a: 'Extract and normalize mission context and safety constraints into standardized context vectors via Telemetry and Tasking interfaces, with formal schema definition and validation logic'; (2) DP4b: 'Apply deterministic rule engine to map normalized context vectors to performance target ranges, with rule provenance, versioning, and rollback capability.' Additionally, add DP4c: 'Coordinate versioning and rollback across distributed nodes with eventual consistency semantics and explicit failure mode handling.' This decomposition aligns with the recommended revisions and makes the implementation strategy more explicit and testable.

assumptions: Context extraction logic is owned by Safety Services (not delegated to upstream module) unless complexity analysis determines otherwise
Rollback coordination uses eventual consistency model rather than strict synchronous coordination to tolerate network latency
SafetyAlert consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE) can tolerate versioned alert schemas with explicit deprecation policies
Worksite safety plan and operational policies are available as structured inputs to rule engine (not free-form documents)
Telemetry and Tasking interfaces provide clean, well-defined input vectors without requiring Safety Services to parse or interpret domain-specific formats
open_risks: Stage 1 context extraction complexity is unknown: if preprocessing is non-trivial, Safety Services may become overloaded and require upstream Context Normalization module. Mitigation: conduct complexity analysis during DP4.1 decomposition.

Distributed versioning and rollback may introduce latency and consistency windows that violate safety-critical timing requirements. Mitigation: define explicit consistency guarantees and test under worst-case network conditions.

SafetyAlert schema evolution may break downstream consumers if versioning strategy is not enforced. Mitigation: establish SafetyAlert as a versioned contract with explicit deprecation policies before implementation.

Rule provenance metadata may become voluminous in high-frequency deployments, creating storage and audit trail management challenges. Mitigation: define retention policies and archival strategy for provenance records.

Tasking interface may provide ambiguous or conflicting mission parameters that complicate constraint extraction. Mitigation: define formal schema for Tasking inputs and validation rules for constraint derivation.
evidence_needed: Formal context vector schema with examples and validation rules (required for FR4.1 verification)
Rule engine specification with determinism proof and provenance metadata format (required for FR4.2 verification)
Distributed versioning and rollback protocol with consistency guarantees and failure mode analysis (required for FR4.3 verification)
SafetyAlert schema with versioning strategy and backward compatibility matrix (required for FR4.4 verification)
Complexity analysis of Stage 1 context extraction to determine if upstream Context Normalization module is needed
Test harness design for injecting context vectors, verifying rule determinism, validating provenance, and confirming rollback behavior
Audit trail format and retention policy for versioning events and baseline updates
Network partition and node failure scenarios for distributed rollback testing
